title: Discrete and Continuous Wigner Functions in Open Quantum Systems: Non-Markovian and Thermodynamic Effects
abstract: The central aim of the thesis is to examine how non-classical resources in finite-dimensional quantum systems can be identified, characterized, and protected for practical use in the presence of realistic noise. Using the discrete Wigner functions (DWFs) framework, we introduce negative quantum states and examine how their Wigner negativity, mana, coherence, and teleportation fidelity evolve under unital and non-unital channels, with particular attention to non-Markovian random-telegraph and amplitude-damping dynamics. We also analyze protection strategies based on weak measurement and quantum measurement reversal, showing that these methods can enhance quantum correlations, reduce fidelity deviation, and improve teleportation performance for two-qubit negative states in memory-bearing environments. Moreover, we demonstrate that certain negative states, derived from phase-space point operators, exhibit greater resilience than Bell states in measures of entanglement under non-Markovian noise. Further, this thesis focuses on developing and implementing quantum circuits for generating these states on superconducting hardware and realizing them for the first time on IBM's ibm-Brisbane device. Their preparation is verified using quantum state tomography, demonstrating high fidelity under realistic noise conditions. We propose a teleportation scheme that leverages one of the two-qubit negative quantum states as a resource. Moreover, these two-qubit negative quantum states are also found to perform better than the Bell states for maximal CHSH violation and Fisher information in noisy conditions. We believe that these negative quantum states have the potential to be used in place of the traditional Bell states in scenarios where non-Markovian errors are prevalent. (continued in the PDF)
summary: This paper investigates how non-classical quantum resources degrade in noisy, non-Markovian environments using the discrete Wigner function formalism. It demonstrates that specific 'negative quantum states' offer superior protection compared to standard Bell states. The research combines theoretical analysis with experimental implementation on superconducting qubits, advancing the practical use of quantum resources in realistic noisy settings.
main_problem: The research aims to characterize, identify, and protect non-classical quantum resources in finite-dimensional quantum systems when subjected to realistic noise and non-Markovian environmental effects.
main_result: Negative quantum states are shown to be more resilient than traditional Bell states against non-Markovian noise, and weak measurement/quantum measurement reversal techniques can enhance quantum correlations and teleportation fidelity.
method: The study employs the discrete Wigner functions (DWFs) framework to analyze state evolution under various open quantum channels, comparing results across Markovian and non-Markovian regimes.
model_or_system: The work focuses on two-qubit quantum systems, utilizing models like the spin-boson and collision models, and is demonstrated experimentally using superconducting hardware (IBM's ibm_brisbane device).
key_observables: Wigner negativity, mana, coherence, teleportation fidelity, concurrence, CHSH violation, and Quantum Fisher Information.
important_parameters: Non-Markovian noise parameters (e.g., $\gamma$, $b$), coupling strengths, and specific state parameters ($p, q$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the applicability of the DWF framework and models the environment using specific noise channels (AD, RTN).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time evolution of Wigner negativity, concurrence, and fidelity for various states under non-Markovian noise, comparing results with and without measurement reversal techniques.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the evolution of non-classical resources under non-Markovian noise, proposes measurement-based protection strategies, and concludes with the experimental realization of these states on superconducting hardware.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it bridges quantum information theory (resource quantification) with open quantum systems dynamics (non-Markovianity), providing concrete, hardware-validated methods for preserving quantum resources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
